schools.summary: Two titles — 'ministers' (hyperetas, 'under-rowers,' the lowest servants at the oar, marking subordination and toil) and 'stewards of the mysteries of God' (entrusted with the gospel's treasures); valued, not over-valued (Henry), neither masters nor despised.
Of a steward one thing is required — that he 'be found faithful' (proved faithful); not brilliance or popularity but fidelity to the trust committed, dispensing the master's goods, not his own.
Calvin: the title of steward is empty without an upright stewardship, whose rule is fidelity.

Being judged of men 'a very small thing' (eis elachiston) — not insensible to good opinion but answerable to his Master only; he does not even rest on his own self-judgment; freeing the minister from partisans' flattery and criticism.
Chrysostom: against the disease of restless prying and judging, which Christ chastised (Matt 7:1).

themes: judged of you a small thing

schools.summary: 'I know nothing against myself' (ouden synoida) — of his apostolic faithfulness, not sinlessness (Calvin); yet not thereby justified, for self-knowledge is fallible; 'he that judgeth me is the Lord,' the one competent Judge.

'Judge nothing before the time' — stop the censorious habit ruining the church (Matt 7:1); wait for the Lord, who reveals the hidden and lays bare the heart's counsels; 'then shall every man have praise of God' — the misjudged steward commended.
Calvin: he reproves hasty, rash judgment, not all discernment.

'Transferred in a figure' (meteschematisa) — Paul applied to himself and Apollos what concerned the real party-leaders, 'for your sakes,' to teach without offence 'not to think of men above that which is written' — esteem of teachers bound to Scripture.
Chrysostom: he argued as if himself the person addressed, the dignity of the named persons restraining wrath.

Three questions demolishing pride — 'who maketh thee to differ?' (diakrinei), 'what hast thou that thou didst not receive?', 'why glory as if not received?'; every distinction a free gift from God, so boasting is folly and ingratitude — the cure for self-conceit.

themes: 'what hast thou that thou didst not receive?'

schools.summary: Keen irony — 'full, rich, reigning as kings, without us,' as if they had reached the summit; a still-carnal church fancying itself perfected; the wistful 'I would to God ye did reign' — the kingdom not yet come, contrasting their triumphalism with the apostles' suffering.

The arena imagery — God set the apostles 'last' (eschatous apedeixen), like condemned gladiators 'appointed to death,' a 'spectacle' (theatron) to world, angels, and men; their suffering reality set against the Corinthians' fancied royalty.

themes: a spectacle to world and angels

schools.summary: Three ironic antitheses — apostles 'fools, weak, despised' vs Corinthians 'wise, strong, honourable' (touching teaching, demeanour, worldly position); the apostles' folly 'for Christ's sake' (Phil 3:7), a chosen reproach; the church's inverted values exposed.

The irony drops to plain reality — 'to this present hour' (achri tes arti horas) the apostles hunger, thirst, go naked, are buffeted, homeless; a graphic picture of Paul's continuing condition, not past trials; shaming the Corinthians' ease.

They 'toil' (kopiomen) with their own hands (tent-making, Acts 18:3; 1 Thess 2:9), not to burden the churches; reviled, they bless; persecuted, they endure — the temper of Christ, returning grace for abuse, rebuking the Corinthians' contention.

'Defamed, we intreat'; 'the filth of the world... the offscouring of all things' (perikatharmata, sweepings/rinsings) — recalling the heathen expiatory victims, the lowest wretches loaded with curses to cleanse a city; the climax of abasement, shaming the Corinthians' pride.

'Not to shame you' (entrepon — the harsh tone suddenly changed) but to warn as beloved children; the sharp irony was a father's discipline, not an enemy's gall (Henry) — faithful enough to wound, loving enough to heal.
Calvin: he spoke not to cover them with shame but with paternal affection.

'Ten thousand instructers' (paidagogous, the replaceable guardian-slaves who led children to school) but only one father; the emphatic 'I' begot them in Christ through the gospel — a unique, unrepeatable relation giving Paul a claim no other teacher has.

'Be ye imitators of me' (mimetai mou ginesthe, keep on becoming) — as children resemble their father (Phil 3:17); chiefly his humility, refusal of party-spirit, and Christlike temper; no arrogance, for the pattern leads through him to Christ.

Timothy sent ahead (Acts 19:21-22), Paul's 'beloved son' in the faith, to remind them of Paul's 'ways in Christ' as he taught 'every where' — a faithful witness recalling them to the apostolic pattern common to all the churches, from which they had strayed.

'Some are puffed up' (ephysiothesan, the same swelling as 3:6) 'as though I would not come' — emboldened in his absence, perhaps reading Timothy's sending as proof he dared not come; the way of false teachers, presuming on the good's absence (Calvin).

themes: as though I would not come

schools.summary: 'I will come shortly, if the Lord will' (ean ho kyrios thelese, James 4:15) — holding his plans under God; he will test not 'the speech' of the puffed-up but 'the power,' the real spiritual authority; not to terrify but to deflate their insolence (Calvin).

'The kingdom of God is not in word but in power' (Matt 3:2) — not empty boasting or eloquence but the mighty energy of the Spirit converting, sanctifying, ruling the heart and building the church; the criterion by which the boasters will be tested.

themes: the kingdom in power not word
not eloquence but the Spirit's energy

schools.summary: 'With a rod, or in love?' (en rhabdo, the father's/schoolmaster's staff, cf Isa 11:4) — Paul gives them the choice; the manner of his coming depends on their repentance, preserving both apostolic authority (he has a rod) and paternal affection (he would rather come gently).
